Just before sunrise, the horizon glows faintly orange while the rest of
the sky is still deep blue. Astronomers call this the civil twilight
band, and it is short-lived at the equator, longer near the poles.

Ancient navigators measured the angle between a star and the horizon to
figure out how far north or south they had traveled. No thread of modern
electronics was involved — just a sextant, a steady hand, and patience.

Some telescopes today are networked through a shared channel so several
observatories can compare notes on the same patch of sky in real time.
